Todd Brown Personal Copy Legends Lock-In Notes
Date: October 17, 2023 - Location: Orlando, Florida
Key: Notes below will contain the initials of the copy legend who is the source of
the idea
● Research
○ Many of the copy legends use some type of feedback from
prospects and buyers to gain more insight.
■ JG: Relies almost exclusively on the results from a simple
one question survey sent to his email list.
● “Which of these would you buy?”
○ Then lists 5-6 options, with hook, and a yes or
no response
○ He sees a direct correlation between the
responses to this survey and the results in
sales from his email promotions.
○ PK: Goes direct to the spy tools to see the top-producing
campaigns within a marketplace. Then, reverse engineers the
copy, idea, promise, etc., to learn everything he needs to know
about what his copy needs to do, say, show, etc. He then looks
at things like: Primary Promise, Big Marketing Idea, and Unique
Mechanisms being used.
■ I like this. Think it’s a shortcut to knowing what copy is
already working to move the market to buy.
■ Sidenote: PK’s mindset is to get to the top of the spy tools
○ RS: Talked about using the following single question on the
customer thank you page: “What almost made you not buy?”
■ DF: Said he routinely uses responses to this question to
optimize his front-end copy.
○ KS: Uses research to identify fresh, new proof points.
■ She fights the “Curse of Sameness” in all areas of her
copy, including the proof points she uses to prove her
claims.
■ She even looks for big, new proof claims which she can
then build her Big Ideas around, and even her campaign
arguments. She uses research to find proof which can
then be used to prove an idea.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 10 --

○ RS: When people wait an hour or more on a coaching call to
ask a question, RK notes the question as something important
to address in marketing/copy.
● Big Idea Development
○ DaF: Using an “In or Out” approach
■ “In” is looking within the product, at the editor/expert, or
within the story of the company/product creation.
■ “Out” is looking outside the product and company, and
looking to something occurring in the marketplace or
general media.
● He builds his campaigns in a way where he can
easily plug-in a new idea/lead. So if his initial idea
doesn’t work well, he can replace it with a new idea
without having the change the majority of the rest of
the copy.
○ DF: All Big Marketing Ideas, Hooks, and copy angles have a
life-span. What worked extremely well a year ago may not work
at all today.
■ We discussed why the famous John Carlton “One legged
golfer” headline would no longer work in today’s golf
market.
● This goes back to TF’s comment about
sophisticated markets requiring more diagnostic,
education-based copy.
○ TF: Being extremely root cause focused and diagnostic during
the research phase often makes it easy to see the Big
Marketing Idea, and it makes writing the copy extremely easy.
○ SM: There are: gimmicks, promises, notions, ideas, and big
ideas.
■ It’s easier to sell, according to Mark Ford, if you have
gimmicks and promises. This doesn’t mean it’s better, just
easier.
■ An idea is just a larger idea about a mechanism or about
a problem that people don’t recognize. The idea is making
a specific argument that you need some particular
product to overcome some particular problem or to tap
into some other mechanism that up until now has been
undiscovered.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 10 --

■ A Big Idea is the same, but is about some bigger societal
issue which is more important and more impactful (i.e.
End of America). And, of course, this has to be timely and
relevant.
● A true big idea has a time and life span. They exist
within a certain time.
○ DD: Big Ideas can often be fouled-up by lesser copywriters who
take it into hyperbolic territory. The value of a Big Marketing
Idea is that it can and should be able to stand on its own
without needing hyperbolic language to prop it up.
○ JG: Look for the hooks and angles which people resonate with,
but that haven’t been used before.
■ He gave the example of how they took a green drink and
focused it around veterans.
■ DaF: Chimed in and shared the idea that JG could have
gone “in” with the big idea by focusing on how they
tweaked and played with the sodium content of the green
drink. This could have produced another idea/hook/angle
they could have gone with.
○ TF: The difference between a big benefit and big idea is that
usually big ideas are paradigm shifting. And usually this
paradigm shift is around something they already believe or
something they want to believe.
■ He gave the examples of End of America and The
Four-Hour Work Week.
● End of America was based on something people
were already believing was going to happen.
● The Four-Hour Work Week was based on
something they wanted to believe was possible.
■ This is why big marketing idea campaigns, when done
correctly, are often passed around and shared with family
and friends. Because it created a paradigm shift for the
initial prospect.
○ RM: The number one problem he sees in the copy which comes
across his desk is that the ideas aren’t big enough, the
promises aren’t compelling enough, and the gimmicks aren’t
gimmicky enough. The copy is playing too small.
● Headlines & Leads
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 10 --

○ JB: Likes to build his primary promises with two core things in
mind:
■ Dream Life
■ Believability
● You need both. The promised payoff has to be big
enough to get the prospect excited (i.e. “the juice is
worth the squeeze”), but also has to be believable.
● One without the other is a loser.
○ I believe too many marketers focus almost
exclusively on making their promises bigger,
more compelling, more audacious; and, often,
lose sight of the believability of what they’re
saying.
■ The more big, bold, and audacious the
primary promise is (i.e. promise of the
dream life), the more work needs to go
into making everything believable.
■ KM: The default lead, when you’re lacking other
approaches or ideas, is the standard Visual Pattern
Interrupt — “Take a look at this picture…”
● SM: Is having great success using memes as an
attention grabber right in the lead.
■ CH: The opening story should serve a strategic purpose
(i.e. grab attention), but it must be congruent with the rest
of the narrative/message. Using a shock story for
shock-sake is foolish if it’s not connected to the rest of the
message. It must naturally connect with what comes next.
● Page Layout
○ RS: Start with mobile page, then develop desktop since mobile
is the primary platform when coming from paid media
■ These are not variations, these are custom pages specific
for the platform.
■ Mobile is often reduced, sometimes containing far less
copy.
○ DF: Customizes the headline, and often much of the copy,
based on the platform.
○ DF: Is having great success with VSL-hybrid pages that have
the headline and sub-headline BELOW the VSL.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 10 --

■ They used Hot Jar to see the flow and order prospects
consume the content in. And they found this was the
order. They’ve tested this with fantastic results.
● Unique Mechanism Development
○ PK: A big proponent of building campaign arguments around
reframing the prospect’s perceived problem as a mere symptom
of a deeper underlying root cause problem. His primary aim is
to, first, get buy-in from the prospect on the new root cause
problem. Then, he solves that new root cause problem with the
introduction of his Unique Mechanism.
■ The key here is building campaigns around introducing
and proving the prospect has a deeper root cause
problem which they’re not addressing, nor are the other
products and offers available to them.
■ And a good Mechanism is one that is on a kindergarten
level in terms of understandability. It’s that simple.
■ Most of the copy legends agreed fully with this.
○ TF: The more sophisticated a market is (i.e. the more they’ve
been exposed to marketing and sales messages), the more
diagnostic the copy needs to be.
■ Think like a doctor of the niche.
■ A bulk of the educational content of the marketing
message should be built around diagnosing the real root
cause of the prospect’s problem. This is the same as what
PK was saying. Educate the prospect on why the problem
they think they have is not the real underlying root cause
problem. Explain how they will only achieve “success”
when they solve the real root cause problem. Then, show
them how this is done with your Unique Mechanism.
● Note: This is dictated by the market. Some markets,
which aren’t nearly as sophisticated, may not
respond well to diagnostic copy. Again, it’s all about
the market and where they’re at.
○ JB: Likes to use two words (already known by the prospect) to
form a new phrase which is used to name the new root cause
problem or to name the Unique Mechanism.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 10 --

■ The example he gave was his campaign which was about
the new root cause problem he named as Leptin
Resistance.
■ Muscle confusion from Beachbody (P90X) is another
example of this.
● In both these instances the two words were,
individually, known by the prospect. But the
combination created a new phrase.
○ RS: Likes to look at why other products aren’t working.
■ The idea being — if customers are still trying to solve a
problem, it means whatever they did before didn’t work for
them. Why not? What’s missing from those products?
What is lacking in their Mechanism?
● Then, using that as a jumping off point, it helps to
see what needs to be within the new Unique
Mechanism.
● This opens possibilities which generally lead to
something which the prospect hasn’t seen before,
and it explains away past failure for the prospect.
○ DF: Unique Mechanism-based campaigns are often much more
evergreen than Big Marketing Idea-based campaigns.
■ This is because Unique Mechanisms can often have
staying power in a market long-term, whereas Big
Marketing Ideas all have an expiration at some point.
● Offer Creation
○ PK: Uses the spy tools again to analyze the top offers within a
marketplace. Then, reverse engineers them to use in his own
offers. He’s not reinventing the wheel. He’s modeling the top
performing campaigns in the marketplace.
○ JG: Looks at the hooks and offers that are working best to his
email list, then reuses those hooks & offers with a new
mechanism to roll-out new promos.
■ In other words, when JG has a winning hook and offer, he
looks for other products which can match the hook and
offer, but have a different Unique Mechanism for
producing the results for prospects.
○ DF: Uses dynamic upsell pricing
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 10 --

■ When someone takes a higher-priced front-end option,
they’re presented with a higher-priced upsell.
○ JG: How you bring customers in is how they’ll behave going
forward. This is why JG is not a big advocate of discounting on
the front-end. It produces discount-oriented buyers.
● Ad Copy
○ RS: Taste vs. Tease
■ Starting with a valuable nugget right away
● DF: After spending tens of millions in testing, he
confirms their Youtube ads based on taste far
outperform the ones based on tease.
● Take the most valuable thing you’re going to say,
and lead with it in the ad.
○ PK: Runs full length VSLs as ad on social then sends to
advertorial.
■ They’re killing it with this approach.
● Optimization
○ DD: Tests five different appeals/angles/ideas for each headline:
■ Overarching benefit headline
■ Story headline
■ More specific headline
■ Three-word Sugarman-style headline
■ Something from left field
○ DaF: Advocates “T Testing” (i.e. horizontal then vertical)
■ Horizontal is testing a variety of ideas, then letting the
data determine the winning idea.
■ Vertical is using the winning idea from the horizontal data,
then testing variations of the winning idea (eg. variations
in the headline copy).
● Misc.
○ CH: Felt strongly the root of all effective copy needs to be deep
empathy for the prospect and their situation.
■ Get into the mind of the prospect, see the world thru their
eyes, experience the emotions they have based on their
current situation.
■ I was surprised to see how many copy legends chimed in
here in passionate agreement with this. A good reminder.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 10 --

○ PK: In mass markets, it’s absolutely essential to use simple,
basic “cave man” language. Everything needs to be
immediately understandable by even the least knowledgeable
and sophisticated prospects.
■ Think brain-dead simple always.
○ DF: Start campaigns off by using the prospect’s vocabulary in
descriptions of problems and outcomes. Then, expand their
vocabulary by introducing them to our internal, proprietary
language. In other words, gradually move the prospect from
their language to ours to understand what we need them to
understand.
○ RS: Gave a good reminder — front-end products should contain
our absolute best stuff, best material. The aim is to wow new
customers with the quality of the front-end product.
■ Remember: The goal of the front-end is not only new
customer acquisition. It’s also the acquisition of new
customers who are likely to ascend and purchase more
and more products from us. This certainly requires
wowing them with the front-end product.
○ PK: The main reason prospects don’t buy is because they don’t
believe the Unique Mechanism will work for them the way we’re
saying it will.
■ This is another reminder of how important believability is
within copy.
■ This is also a reminder that sometimes you’re better
compressing your primary promise if that’s what it takes to
make it believable. You’re better off having a believable
smaller promise than you are an unbelievable bigger
promise.
○ TF: Smart entrepreneurs will not create the product until they’ve
identified the root cause problem it’s going to solve, as well as
the Unique Mechanism. The product should be built around
those two.
■ In the supplement space, for example, this is the
equivalent of identifying the root cause of the problem the
supplement is going to solve. Then identify the necessary
formulation (i.e. Unique Mechanism) needed to solve that
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 10 --

root cause problem. And then creating the supplement
based on the needed formula.
■ Marketing first, then product creation.
○ SW: Inputs all his competitive research (benefits, promise,
mechanism) into AI, then asks it to tell him what’s not stated but
implied about the benefits, problems, promise, etc.
■ This gives him a deeper understanding of what’s
happening on a deeper level with the copy.
○ DF: Invested 300K in copywriters trying to beat JG’s OLOF
upsell strategy, and nothing has beat it.
■ Offer, length, opening, format
● First minute to minute and half to resell the prospect
on the product they just purchased.
■ They also tested removing the big “Wait. Your order isn’t
finished processing” area, and conversions tanked.
● Keep in mind, this is based on a sophisticated older
market. Worth testing in other markets.
■ They’re also subtly teasing the upsell in their front-end
copy. This is an advanced strategy.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 10 --

www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 10 --
